tasks.detail: User request. The lexer accepts both ASCII and fancy forms; the editor should live-convert the ASCII to the symbol. Must be word-boundary safe (don't break Int/min/etc.).

ASCII op-pairs (<= >= != =>) convert on 2nd char; word mnemonics (in/notin/implies/impliedby/when/forall/all/exists/any/delta/and/or/not/mapsto/to/langle/rangle/leq/geq/neq/times/cdot/cup/cap/subseteq/emptyset) convert when a non-word char follows the COMPLETE preceding word — word-boundary safe (Int/min/Coining/band/auto/cannot/xin do NOT convert). tasks.title: evident fmt — a gofmt-style pretty-printer that rewrites a program to an equivalent, idiomatic one
tasks.detail:
User request: a 'gofmt for Evident'. Parse → AST → pretty-print a SEMANTICALLY EQUIVALENT program that looks better; must be idempotent (fmt∘fmt = fmt) and round-trip (re-parses, ./test.sh stays green). Likely a Rust 'evident fmt <file> [--write]' command reusing the parser + a pretty-printer over core/ast.rs (the AST Display is the seed). Target rules (start with the user's, align with docs/evident-purism.md + the idioms in CLAUDE.md):
 (1) Line length — wrap long lines (~88 cols) sensibly; consistent indentation.
 (2) Vertical spacing / newlines — group decls vs constraints; blank line between sections.
 (3) Minimize ∧ — rewrite 'A ⇒ (B ∧ C)' and conjunction chains into the indented implies-block / one-constraint-per-line-at-same-tab-level form (newline = ∧).
 (4) Prefer Δ in fsm models — rewrite 'var = _var + step' carries into '¬is_first_tick ⇒ Δvar = step' (honor the gotchas: decl outside the guard; single-line ⇒, or an indented block grouping deltas).
 (5) Reduce expression duplication — factor a repeated subexpression into a named chained-membership binding 'name ∈ T = expr' and reference it.
 (6) Drop type annotations the inference can recover; use chained-membership (e.g. '0 ≤ x ∈ Int ≤ 5'); element-iteration ('∀ e ∈ s') over '∀ i ∈ {0..#s-1}' index loops; coindexed/edges over parallel-seq loops; avoid ternary chains; keep 'type main' compact.
The purism doc is the authority on 'good Evident'; the formatter should produce purism-CLEAN output. Open question to settle with the user: exact column width, and how aggressive (5) should be.

log.note: evident fmt: gofmt-style, comment-preserving, self-verifying formatter (runtime/src/fmt.rs). Line/token normalizer (NOT AST-rebuild — comments aren't in the AST, would be deleted). Fixes indentation to 4-space grid via structural nesting stack, trims trailing ws, collapses blank runs; preserves bracket-continuation alignment verbatim. format_source re-tokenizes its output and refuses (Err, file untouched) unless token-equivalent + idempotent. CLI: evident fmt <file>... [--write|-w] [--check]. 9 unit tests + corpus test (all .ev under examples/stdlib/packages format clean, fixed-point, mangle→canonical). ./test.sh green (263). Skipped the AST-rewrite transforms (and-minimize / drop-annotations / ternary-to-delta): unsafe under comment preservation. Branch worktree-agent-a51e4ed8afefe3bdc, commit 39f6287.

fmt refuses or corrupts on ragged indentation — its core advertised use. (1) An over-indented line BEFORE a same-line ⇒ line (e.g. 'count ∈ Int' at 8 spaces, then 'is_first_tick ⇒ count = 0' at 2 spaces) makes reindent change the relative indent ordering, so the self-check fires 'formatter changed the token stream — refusing to emit' and the file is left UNCHANGED (exit 1). Repro: /tmp/messy.ev (the canonical counter), and the canonical multi-delta accumulate block from CLAUDE.md mis-indented one level. (2) WORSE: when a sibling line is over-indented relative to the line above it, fmt 'succeeds' (exit 0) but PRESERVES the mistake as fake nesting — '  count ∈ Int' then '      is_first_tick ⇒ count = 0' comes out with the guard at 8-space depth, nested UNDER count as if a child block, changing the program's apparent structure. Root cause (runtime/src/fmt.rs reindent): the nesting stack is purely indentation-ordering-based and only canonicalizes UNIFORM over/under-indent; it cannot repair INCONSISTENT indentation, which is exactly the mess users run a formatter to fix. Layout-only is an acceptable scope, but it must at minimum re-indent ragged sibling lines to the same depth and never refuse on (or silently mis-nest) realistic messy input.

tasks.title: True minimal, named UNSAT core over asserted terms (not source lines, not type-decls): mirror Z3's minimal core so 'removing any one makes it solvable' is actually true. Current core includes 'x ∈ Int' declarations and is non-minimal.

log.note: delta-debug skips pure decls; core is now the real conflicting constraints only

UNSAT core is UNSOUND for redundant constraints on a shared variable: the displayed set is not itself unsatisfiable. Test: claim {x∈Int,y∈Int, x>3, x>5, y>5, y<100, x+y<10}. IDE shows core = {line6 y>5, line8 x+y<10}, claiming 'removing any one makes it solvable'. But {y>5, x+y<10} alone is SAT (z3 model x=3,y=6) — it is NOT a valid unsat core; it dropped BOTH x-lower-bounds (x>3 AND x>5). Z3's real minimal core is {x>5, y>5, x+y<10}. The minimizer is greedily deleting constraints without re-checking the residual is still UNSAT (or mishandling the x>3/x>5 redundancy by dropping both). A core a verification engineer trusts MUST itself be unsatisfiable; this one is satisfiable. The simple #73 case now passes, but minimality is unsound the moment two constraints bound the same var. Fix: use z3 assert_and_track + unsat_core (it returns {x>5,y>5,sum}), or deletion-based minimization that re-solves and keeps the set UNSAT at every step.
deletion-based minimization → genuine minimal unsat core; verified on the redundant case

tasks.title: Code-quality linter for ide/ + viz/: file/function length, free-function count, coupling
tasks.detail: USER REQUEST: the IDE codebase is getting unwieldy; add linters enforcing: (1) max file line count (~500, per CLAUDE.md), (2) max function/method line count (~70), (3) max number of module-level functions outside classes per file (a 'logic not encapsulated' smell), (4) module↔class separation / tight-coupling heuristic (e.g. a module-level function reaching into another module's internals, or high cross-module import fan-out). Standalone CLI that scans ide/*.py + viz/*.py (+ line-count for app.js), reports violations grouped by rule, exits non-zero for CI. Configurable thresholds at top. Report the CURRENT baseline violations.

log.note: Built ide/lint.py (stdlib-ast, standalone CLI from repo root). 4 configurable rules: file<=500, def<=70, <=12 module-level funcs, coupling-score<=8 (sibling-module imports + 2*private cross-module accesses; stdlib/3rd-party imports excluded -> low false positives). Groups by rule w/ path:line, --quiet summary, '# noqa: lint' suppression, non-zero exit to gate CI. Dogfoods clean. Baseline on current tree: 31 violations = 6 files>500 (ide/task.py 582, app.js 996, render_basin_map 826, render_fixedpoint_map 650, render_morse_graph 548, render_phase_portrait 528) + 18 funcs>70 (worst: render_state_graph.render 164, render_basin_map._discrete_basins 150, render_orbit_scatter.render 149) + 7 files>12 free-funcs (render_fixedpoint_map 28, render_basin_map 25, ide/task.py 22). Rule4 coupling: 0 - viz/ide files are standalone (stdlib/matplotlib), not tangled. Genuine split candidates: render_basin_map.py + render_fixedpoint_map.py (both over file AND free-func limits w/ huge defs). Doc: ## Linting in new ide/README.md. Committed in worktree (428b7983).

tasks.title: Guided 'learn the ideas' affordance, not just the symbols — a short in-tool explainer / walkthrough that teaches what claim / fsm / is_first_tick / Δ / a difference-equation MEAN as concepts. The symbol palette (great) tells me how to TYPE Δ ('forward difference') but not what a difference equation is or why is_first_tick exists. I opened all the FSM samples and could read the GLYPHS but still couldn't explain to a friend what 'fsm counter' is doing. Wall: I wanted to go from 'I can see it ramps to 5' to 'I understand WHY count = (is_first_tick ? 0 : _count+1) makes it ramp' entirely in-tool, and there's nowhere to learn that. Codecademy/Brilliant teach a concept beside the first example; here the samples assume I already know the model. Idea: a collapsible 'how this works' note per sample, or a one-time 'first program' tour that annotates the counter line-by-line in plain English.

Sudoku witness should draw the filled n×n DIGIT grid, not a flat 16-wide index→value strip. The queen-board fabrication is correctly gone (good), but for the 4×4 sudoku sample the witness renders as one row of 16 cells [1,2,3,4,4,3,1,2,...] plus a raw-JSON 'boxes' row. As a newcomer I literally cannot read the Sudoku solution out of a 16-long strip — I'd have to mentally wrap it into 4 rows. Draw it as a 4×4 grid of digits (the domain picture), the way the N-queens board now does. The same renderer that detects 'this is a board' should reshape #cell=16 into 4×4.

Pretty-print record-Seq witnesses instead of raw JSON. subset-sum shows items as [{"take":false,"weight":3},{"take":true,"weight":11}] and sudoku shows boxes as [{"a":1,"b":2,...}]. A newcomer reading a witness has to parse JSON in their head. Render a small table (columns take|weight, one row per item, the chosen rows highlighted) so I can SEE which items were picked and that their weights sum to the target. Domain picture, not the encoding.

log.note: DUPLICATE — shipped as #196 (record-Seq witness as a table, no JSON); approve to close

tasks.title: The ⊢verify result should teach what an invariant IS, not just pass/fail. Right now 'count ≤ 5' → '✓ proven on all 6 reachable states' and 'count ≤ 3' → '✗ violated — counterexample count=4' (excellent, this is the best teaching moment in the whole tool). Push it further: on ✗, offer a one-click 'show me the trace to count=4' that highlights the path in the time-series/state-graph; on ✓, a tiny 'an invariant is a property true in EVERY reachable state — you just proved one' tooltip the first time. That turns the verify box from a checker into a teacher.

Persist editor + verify input across refresh, and a Save/share affordance. I typed a 'claim over' from scratch and a verify assertion; if I refresh I lose both (the editor resets to the default sample). Every editor I use (even a scratchpad) survives reload via localStorage. Also no way to share a model — I'd want a 'copy link'/'export .ev' so I can show a friend the win. Wall: I wanted to keep my over-constrained example to come back to and had nowhere to put it.

log.note: DUPLICATE — shipped as #174 (buffer persists across reload); approve to close

tasks.title: Verify box: stale assertion text persists across sample switches. After typing 'mode != Vending' on vending then switching to counter, the verify input still held 'mode != Vending' over the counter. Minor, but combined with concern #100 (placeholder over-promises) it means the verify input never feels 'fresh' for the current program. Clear (or re-default) the verify input on program switch.

Title bar shows the wrong .ev name for multi-decl claim samples — toposort and Edge-typed samples show 'Edge.ev' (the first 'type' decl) instead of the claim/sample name. Cosmetic but a newcomer trusts the title to tell them what they're looking at; 'Edge.ev' for a toposort claim is confusing. Name the file after the entry claim or the sample's menu label.

Exact bounds via z3 Optimize for the solution_space view (essential): replace the BFS-sample min/max with z3 Optimize.maximize/minimize over the constraint set per numeric var. Then 'exact' is provable even when the reachable-state BFS is capped, and the convergent-vs-divergent capped-label ambiguity (concern #112) disappears — Optimize gives the true sup/inf, with 'unbounded' when Optimize says so. This is the single change that makes the lead view's 'solved, not simulated' claim true.

True feasible-region solve for the solution_space right panel (essential): instead of a bounding-box rectangle (concern #113), grid the 2 principal vars' value-space and per cell solve 'is (x,y) extensible to a full satisfying assignment?' — shade feasible cells, outline the actual region. For FSMs, the reachable-set scatter; for claims, the constraint's true solution set. This is the rigorous answer to 'show the boundaries of the model'.

Temporal property checking in the verify console (important): extend assert-and-check (#79, safety done) to 'always P', 'eventually P', 'P leads-to Q' over the reachable transition graph, returning a counterexample TRACE (the path of states) when false — not just a single state. TLA+ checks temporal formulas and gives a trace; right now 'eventually done' returns '✕ write var op value'. Liveness on the BFS graph (lasso detection for 'eventually') is the next tier after safety.

Counterexample as a TRACE, not just an endpoint (important): when verify refutes 'count ≤ 4' it returns 'counterexample count=5' — the violating STATE. A verification engineer wants the PATH to it (the sequence of states/ticks that reaches the violation), like TLA+'s error trace. Surface the BFS path from an initial state to the counterexample state, steppable. This also feeds the basin/reachability story.

log.note: scrubbable counterexample trace stepper (#198 alias; also covers #13/#141): ◀/▶ stepper under verify row, full assignment per step, violation flag on final

tasks.title: Indexed/field-element pins in solve-for-X (important): pinning col[0]=0 now honestly errors ('can't pin an indexed/field element yet') instead of silently producing a contradictory witness — good. But the capability is still missing. Support col[0]=0, grid[1][2]=3, rec.field=v by adding the equality as a tracked assertion on the indexed Z3 leaf. N-queens with col[0]=0 should then return UNSAT (provably, no 4-queens solution has col[0]=0).

Enumerate ALL solutions with scope + symmetry breaking (important): the 'all ⊨*' button exists but I want Alloy-style control — a scope/bound I set, symmetry breaking so I'm not drowning in isomorphic witnesses (4-queens has 2 solutions; N-queens scales), and a count. 'Show me every distinct model up to symmetry, bounded at N' is the daily Alloy move; right now I get one witness + a re-solve.

Export / interop (important): I cannot get the model OUT. I want to dump the SMT-LIB the IDE feeds z3 (to take into z3 directly for unsat cores / Optimize), copy a witness as text/JSON, and export the current diagram PNG/SVG for a paper. Every solver I trust (z3 -smt2, Alloy's export) lets me leave with the artifact; here the model is trapped in the browser.

log.note: DUPLICATE — shipped as #200 (⧉ SMT-LIB export); approve to close

tasks.title: Model diff: compare two programs / two reachable sets side by side (important): change a constraint and see what appeared/vanished in the reachable set or the solution space — the relational analog of a diff. I kept wanting to A/B 'with bound vs without bound' and had to eyeball two separate renders from memory. Alloy lets me step instances; I want 'states added (green) / removed (red)' between rev A and rev B.

Ad-hoc query console against the current model (nice-to-have): type a constraint/assertion and evaluate it WITHOUT editing the source — e.g. 'count = 3 ∧ done' → SAT/UNSAT, or 'is there a reachable state with stock=0 ∧ vault>5'. The verify box does invariants over the reachable set; I also want one-shot existence queries against the constraint relation, z3-repl style.

Progress indicator + interruptibility for slow structure analysis (nice-to-have): oscillator still takes ~4.2s and the UI shows no 'computing…' state during it — it looks frozen. Show a spinner/elapsed-time on the structure card while the analysis runs, and let me cancel. Pairs with the latency concern #75 (still >300ms on real-valued FSMs).

log.note: DUPLICATE — shipped as #202 (elapsed/progress counter); approve to close

tasks.title: Headline-view samples open on their named view, not always solution_space (nice-to-have): samples labelled '(FSM, cobweb)', '(FSM, basin_map)', '(FSM, scatter_matrix)' now all open on solution_space (the universal numeric lead). basin_map must not assert a single attractor on nondeterministic systems (from concern #76): bistable (gambler's ruin, step∈{-1,0,1}, branching ×3) paints x=1..5 solid blue (→0) and only x=6 red, with a legend 'terminal basin → (0) fixed pt'. From an interior state the walk can reach EITHER wall, so those states are in BOTH basins. A single color asserts a deterministic attractor the nondeterminism denies. Fix: make basin_map N/A for branching>=2 systems, OR show per-state basin membership as a distribution (two-tone / P(→0) vs P(→6)).

Provenance: 'why is this state reachable?' (nice-to-have): given a reachable state in the solution_space scatter or state_graph, show the path/constraints that admit it — click a dot, get the witnessing trajectory or the satisfying assignment. The relational debugger's 'how did we get here' that turns a picture into something I can interrogate.

Inductive-invariant proving (nice-to-have): the verify console checks an invariant over the BFS-reachable set (bounded). For an unbounded/capped FSM that's not a proof. Offer a z3-backed inductive check: 'is P preserved by the transition relation from any P-state?' (base + step), so 'always P' can be PROVEN for infinite-state systems, not just sampled — the TLA+/Z3 inductive-invariant move.
Render raw CLAIMS (not just FSMs): feasible-region/solution-space via solve

tasks.title: FSM bounds via z3 Optimize over K-step unrolling — provably exact, not sampled
tasks.detail: USER + Ana #117: solution_space bounds are min/max over the BFS sample (exact only when finite/non-capped). Make them PROVABLY exact: unroll the transition relation K steps from init, z3 Optimize each var over the trace → exact bounds over the K-horizon. When K-step and 2K-step bounds agree, the bound is the true fixpoint (the user's 'compose the FSM with itself; 2-run==5-run' idea). Label 'proven over K steps' / 'exact (fixpoint)'.

log.note: solved_bounds: z3 Optimize over k-step unroll; exact-fixpoint vs proven-over-horizon

tasks.title: Language: __var (two-tick history) + ΔΔ second-difference operator
tasks.detail: USER request. Support __var (value two ticks ago) and ΔΔx (second forward difference = Δ(Δx) = x − 2·_x + __x), so a second-order system (the damped oscillator) can be written in pos alone, eliminating the explicit vel state. VERIFIED: pos-only oscillator with ΔΔ reproduces the 2-var version to 2e-14. Spans lexer→trampoline: nested-Δ desugar, prev_of recursion, inject __var decls + is_second_tick, two-tick carry in the trampoline, two-tick bootstrap.

log.note: DUPLICATE — shipped as #158 (__var + ΔΔ second difference); approve to close

tasks.title: Counterexample TRACE, not just the offending state. ⊢verify on counter with 'count ≤ 3' correctly returns '✗ violated — counterexample count=4', but that is a single STATE. TLA+ gives the full path — init → tick → … → the violating state — so I can see HOW the system gets there. For a safety invariant, surface the shortest reachable trajectory that reaches the violating state (BFS path through the state graph), rendered as a step/scrub-able trace. The state graph + reachability_tree already have the edges; thread the witness path through them and let me step it in time. Without the trace, 'count=4' tells me WHAT but not WHY/WHEN.

log.note: DUPLICATE — shipped as #120 (counterexample TRACE); approve to close

tasks.title: Temporal-property checking (liveness), not just safety invariants. ⊢verify checks 'always P' (a state predicate over the reachable set). I also reach for 'eventually Q', 'P leads-to Q', and fairness — the TLA+ temporal operators. e.g. on traffic: 'light = Green eventually holds', 'Coining leads-to Vending'. Over a finite reachable graph these are decidable (a cycle/lasso search). Add ◇ / leads-to / fairness to the verify box; today it can only assert □ (always).

Diff two models / two reachable sets — the relational analog of a code diff. When I tighten a constraint (drop counter's upper bound, change a step), I want to SEE what reachable states appeared or vanished, and how the bounds/fixed-points moved, side by side. Today I edit and the whole picture replaces; I have to remember the old one. Z3 incremental + a snapshot of the previous analysis would make 'what did this constraint change' a one-click comparison.

Ad-hoc query / assertion console against the CURRENT model, beyond the single-line ⊢verify invariant box. I want to type an arbitrary constraint or ∃-query ('∃ a tick where balance=5 ∧ stock=0', 'is there a reachable state with mode=Vending ∧ vault<2') and evaluate it without editing the source — the Z3 incremental-assert workflow. The verify box does □(predicate) only; a real query console (∃/∀, multiple vars, count solutions) is the interrogation surface Alloy's evaluator gives me.

Progress indicator + cancel for slow analysis: real-valued FSMs (oscillator ~4.5s, fourvar) and the k-step Optimize unrolling have NO progress feedback — top bar shows 'computing…' but the user can't tell if it's 1s or 30s, can't cancel, and the diagram sits dimmed. Add a determinate-ish progress (e.g. 'unrolling step 14/24', 'solving bounds…') and a Cancel button. Every serious solver UI (Alloy's progress, a JetBrains background-task bar) shows this.

Click/hover the solution_space reachable-set points to inspect the underlying state. The vending (balance,stock) scatter and oscillator (pos,vel) spiral plot REAL reachable states, but they're inert — I can't click a point to see its full tuple (balance,stock,vault), can't hover for coordinates, can't see which transitions touch it. The diagram is a poster, not a surface. Every debugger lets me click a node.

Export / save / name the current model + share a link. localStorage persists across reload (good) but there is no 'export .ev', no 'copy shareable link', no named slots — I built an over-constrained repro and wanted to keep it AND show a colleague. (Mirrors Sam #109.)

Compare two runs / pin a diagram side-by-side. I wanted to put the BROKEN counter (2 dropped) next to the fixed one to SEE the difference in the solution_space, and to compare two SAT witnesses (re-solve gives a new one but the old one is gone). Add a 'pin'/'compare' that holds diagram A while I edit to B and shows them side by side.

Inline error squiggle at the offending token + dropped-constraint underline in the editor. Dropped constraints are surfaced honestly in the banner/console (great) and name the exact line, but the EDITOR itself shows no squiggle/underline at 'kount' (the misspelled var). I have to read the console and map line numbers back. Every editor underlines the bad token in red. (Mirrors my #42.)

log.note: Dropped constraints now mark their source line in the editor: amber gutter cell + line tint + tooltip (the desugared expr). Chose FALLBACK (frontend/server token-overlap heuristic, NO runtime change) — exact threading onto BodyItem::Constraint had ~48 match sites (many nested Constraint(Expr::Binary(...)) patterns). server.py _dropped_locs weights shared identifiers by 1/source-freq so the rarest/undeclared name (the typo) pins the line. Gate: ¬is_first_tick⇒Δcount=stp → dropped=1, dropped_locs=[4], browser marker on line 4; two-typo→[6,7]; clean→[]; clears on fix. ./test.sh green (265 tests, lint 53≤53).

tasks.title: viz: support two-tick (__var) FSMs — ΔΔ models mis-render as nondeterministic
tasks.detail: The ΔΔ/__var runtime feature (#158) is correct (effect-run reproduces the oscillator exactly), BUT the IDE viz mis-reads it: a pos-only ΔΔ oscillator shows banner 'Nondeterministic — 64 successors' and bounds [-1674,60] instead of [-35,60], because (a) export_transition's schema lists only pos (prev=_pos), hiding the __pos history + is_second_tick, and (b) evident_viz.successor/reachable step ONE tick, leaving __pos free. Fix: export should expose history DEPTH (pos→_pos→__pos) + is_second_tick; evident_viz must carry TWO ticks (state representation = (_state, __state)) in successor/reachable/solved_bounds so a second-order difference equation visualizes correctly. THEN add the elegant pos-only oscillator (ΔΔ) sample.

tasks.title: Hover-to-learn glossary on the jargon (fsm, claim, ∈, Δ, ⇒, is_first_tick, 'Driven pipeline', 'Cyclic', 'fixed point', 'reachable set', 'inductive invariant'). concerns.title: fmt refuses or corrupts on ragged indentation — its core advertised use. (1) An over-indented line BEFORE a same-line ⇒ line (e.g. 'count ∈ Int' at 8 spaces, then 'is_first_tick ⇒ count = 0' at 2 spaces) makes reindent change the relative indent ordering, so the self-check fires 'formatter changed the token stream — refusing to emit' and the file is left UNCHANGED (exit 1). Repro: /tmp/messy.ev (the canonical counter), and the canonical multi-delta accumulate block from CLAUDE.md mis-indented one level. (2) WORSE: when a sibling line is over-indented relative to the line above it, fmt 'succeeds' (exit 0) but PRESERVES the mistake as fake nesting — '  count ∈ Int' then '      is_first_tick ⇒ count = 0' comes out with the guard at 8-space depth, nested UNDER count as if a child block, changing the program's apparent structure. Root cause (runtime/src/fmt.rs reindent): the nesting stack is purely indentation-ordering-based and only canonicalizes UNIFORM over/under-indent; it cannot repair INCONSISTENT indentation, which is exactly the mess users run a formatter to fix. Layout-only is an acceptable scope, but it must at minimum re-indent ragged sibling lines to the same depth and never refuse on (or silently mis-nest) realistic messy input.

Correct for N-queens, but the queen-board renderer is OVER-APPLIED to non-queens witnesses — fabrication (violates Part 4.4 'never invent structure'). concerns.title: UNSAT core is UNSOUND for redundant constraints on a shared variable: the displayed set is not itself unsatisfiable. Test: claim {x∈Int,y∈Int, x>3, x>5, y>5, y<100, x+y<10}. IDE shows core = {line6 y>5, line8 x+y<10}, claiming 'removing any one makes it solvable'. But {y>5, x+y<10} alone is SAT (z3 model x=3,y=6) — it is NOT a valid unsat core; it dropped BOTH x-lower-bounds (x>3 AND x>5). Z3's real minimal core is {x>5, y>5, x+y<10}. The minimizer is greedily deleting constraints without re-checking the residual is still UNSAT (or mishandling the x>3/x>5 redundancy by dropping both). A core a verification engineer trusts MUST itself be unsatisfiable; this one is satisfiable. The simple #73 case now passes, but minimality is unsound the moment two constraints bound the same var. Fix: use z3 assert_and_track + unsat_core (it returns {x>5,y>5,sum}), or deletion-based minimization that re-solves and keeps the set UNSAT at every step.

solution_space headline 'boundaries solved, not simulated' is FALSE: the per-variable min/max bars are computed by min()/max() over the BFS-sampled reachable states (viz/evident_viz.py solution_structure lines 711-716), NOT by z3 Optimize. For FINITE reachable sets (counter/vending/traffic) the BFS exhausts the set so 'exact' is correct, but for CAPPED sets (oscillator, fourvar) the bar is the min/max of the first 400 sampled states — a simulation. Worse, the '≥ lower bound (capped)' framing is BACKWARDS for convergent dynamics: a damped oscillator's sampled extremes (early, wide swings) are the WIDEST the orbit ever is, so the true reachable range is NARROWER, not '≥'. The single capped label can't be sound for both convergent and divergent systems. Task #97 explicitly required z3 Optimize 'not run-observed'; this is run-observed and the title claims otherwise. A verification tool must not print 'solved' over a sampled quantity.
concerns.detail: screenshot 02-oscillator-solution-space.png; source viz/evident_viz.py:711-716 + render_solution_space.py title line 97

concerns.title: solution_space right-panel dashed 'boundary' box FABRICATES a rectangular feasible region. It draws Rectangle(bounds[vx] x bounds[vy]) — the bounding box of the projection — and labels it 'feasible region ... the SET'. But the actual feasible set is the scattered points; interior cells of that rectangle are NOT reachable (vending: (balance=2,stock=2),(3,2),(4,2) are empty inside the [0,5]x[0,3] box). A verification engineer reading 'feasible region' + a rectangle outline infers every point in the rectangle is feasible — false. This is the fabricate-structure hazard (Briefing 4.4): the outline asserts a region the model never enters at the corners. The real dots are drawn faithfully on top, which softens it, but the dashed box should be removed or replaced by a true region outline (convex hull of reachable pts, or a per-cell feasible/infeasible solve).
concerns.detail: screenshot 03b-vending-diagram.png; render_solution_space.py add_patch(Rectangle...) ~line 83

concerns.title: solution_space right panel says 'the SET, not one orbit' even for DETERMINISTIC systems where the reachable set IS exactly one orbit. The oscillator panel plots a single inward spiral (one trajectory's points) and labels it 'the SET, not one orbit' — but a deterministic driven system's reachable set is precisely that one orbit. The label is only truthful for nondeterministic systems (vending). For deterministic ones it overstates: it's the same trajectory the phase_portrait draws, just rendered as disconnected points. Either condition the label on branching>=2, or for deterministic systems relabel as 'the reachable orbit (= the whole set, since deterministic)'.
concerns.detail: screenshot 02-oscillator-solution-space.png right panel

concerns.title: solution_space lead view degenerates to a single bar for enum-dominated systems and DROPS the categorical dimension. traffic (light:enum, timer:int) shows only a 'timer ∈ [0,2]' bar and NO right-hand feasible-region panel, because the panel only plots numeric vars. concerns.title: solved_bounds 'exact (fixpoint)' is justified by k-step and 2k-step Optimize bounds AGREEING (k=12..16). That is strong evidence of closure and airtight for monotone/bounded recurrences (counter reaches its fixpoint by tick 5 ≪ k), but it is a 2k-HORIZON agreement, not an inductive proof: a variable that stays constant for 2k ticks then jumps would be wrongly stamped 'exact'. For a tool that prints 'z3-PROVEN exact', either (a) add an inductive check — assert the k-step reachable envelope is closed under one more application of T (∀ state in env, T(state) ∈ env) and prove it UNSAT-to-escape — or (b) soften the word to 'exact over the explored horizon (k=N, stable at 2k)'. Today's label is correct on every shipped sample but the guarantee is weaker than the word 'PROVEN' implies.
concerns.detail: viz/evident_viz.py solved_bounds: exact = (b1==b2); render_solution_space title 'z3-proven exact (fixpoint)'

The task asked for a REAL view — (a) per-element/per-position feasible-value cells (for each Seq[i], z3 SAT 'can this position take value v given the rest?'), and/or (b) ENUMERATE k distinct witnesses with symmetry breaking, rendered as the domain picture. Neither is present. The single ⊨ Solve witness is still the only interrogation move for exactly the claims a verification engineer most wants to SEE the solution space of. (The worker_done log note for this task is mis-pasted — it's about ΔΔ nested-delta desugar, unrelated to claim_space.) Not done.

Multi-claim/-type files break the ANALYZE path (not solve): a claim file with ANY supporting decl errors out. Live: toposort (has 'type Edge' + 'claim toposort') and sudoku (has 'Box' + 'sudoku') both show banner 'source has an error — fix it to refresh the analysis' and the diagram goes STALE, with bottom console 'export: ambiguous: 2 candidate claims ([Edge, toposort]) — name one with export <file> <claim> is not yet supported; the file must contain exactly one top-level claim'. ⊨ Solve still works (it picks the obvious entry claim), so the analyzer and solver DISAGREE on whether the file is valid. This means #133 (render raw claims) only works for single-decl files — the moment a claim has an idiomatic helper type, the solution-space diagram cannot render. Two SHIPPED samples (toposort, sudoku) load showing an error banner. Fix: the analyzer should pick the same entry claim the solver does (the last/only non-helper top-level claim, or the one matching the filename), not refuse on multi-decl files.

concerns.title: Verify box rejects the chained-range invariant '0 ≤ x ≤ 6' with '✕ write var op value (e.g. count ≤ 5)' — it only accepts a single 'var op value'. A range is the MOST natural invariant shape (a bound is two-sided), and '0 ≤ x ≤ 6' is valid Evident chained-comparison syntax, so a user types exactly what the language allows and the verify box refuses it. Single-sided 'x ≤ 6' works ('✓ proven on all 7 reachable states'). Accept chained ranges (and ideally conjunctions like 'x ≥ 0 ∧ x ≤ 6') in the verify parser.
concerns.detail: bistable loaded; screenshot 14-verify.png; '0 ≤ x ≤ 6' rejected, 'x ≤ 6' proven

concerns.title: Sample-switch / edit intermittently redirects the browser from :5173 to a dead :5273, stranding the client on a port where /api/analyze returns ERR_CONNECTION_REFUSED. Header then reads 'backend down' / 'backend unavailable — the solver isn't responding' and ALL annotations + diagrams silently freeze (no dropped-constraint marker, no parse marker) because they are computed server-side. The :5173 backend itself is healthy (curl /api/analyze returns full payload). A hard reload to :5173 recovers. concerns.title: SMT-LIB export (#200) constant-folds equality-pinned vars, losing the variable. A claim with 'x ∈ Int; x>10; x<5; x=7' exports '(assert (> 7 10)) (assert (< 7 5)) (assert (= 7 7))' with NO '(declare-fun x () Int)' — x was substituted away by the encoder before dump. Handing this to z3 for its own (get-unsat-core) gives three ground literals, not my relational model. Faithful when vars are free (free_x exports declare-fun x/y + all asserts intact) and for the counter FSM (verified re-runnable in z3, returns sat+model), so the dump is real — but for a claim that pins a var to a constant it's z3's simplified residue, not the model I wrote. Export should dump the pre-simplification assertions (declare every named var, keep x=7 as an assertion).
concerns.detail: POST /api/smtlib with claim 'contradiction' vs 'free_x'; compared outputs. Counter FSM export verified through real z3 (/tmp/ana-counter.smt2 -> sat).

concerns.title: SMT-LIB export refuses unsat_*/sat_*-named claims: POST /api/smtlib for 'claim unsat_demo {...}' returns {ok:false, error:'no claims found to export'}; renaming to 'contradiction' exports fine. The exporter inherits the FSM-discovery skip of sat_*/unsat_* names, but those are EXACTLY the assertion claims a verification engineer most wants to hand to z3 (to get z3's own minimal unsat core / try a different tactic). Export should include sat_*/unsat_* claims.
concerns.detail: POST /api/smtlib, unsat_demo vs contradiction. Footer also showed 'no claims found to export'.

concerns.title: Latency on multi-variable / unbounded-driver systems blows the ≲300ms promise badly and silently: a 4-var driven FSM (one unbounded driver a, BFS caps at 400) took 8.1s on /api/analyze, with NO progress indicator (the UI looks frozen for 8 seconds). concerns.title: Query-console TRACE mislabels its witness as a violation. Ran '⊨? query: timer = 2 ∧ light = Green' on traffic — the init→witness trace appears and scrubs correctly (delight), but its final step is flagged '● violation here' and the state carries class 'trace-state bad'. For an existential reachability query the target state is the GOAL, not a violated invariant — that wording/styling is carried over from the ⊢verify counterexample path. A reachability witness should read 'reached here' / styled as a goal (green), reserving 'violation'/red for ⊢verify refutations. Z3/TLA+ never call a sought witness a violation.
concerns.detail: where: trace-head after clicking 'find' on traffic; DOM: span.trace-flag '● violation here' + div.trace-state.bad 'light = Green timer = 2'
